Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
MS-1
• The discussion section has been updated to reflect the changes in the algorithm.
Updates in Version 1.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Kaposi Sarcoma from Version 2.2025 include:
Global
• References were updated throughout the Guidelines.
KS-1
• Diagnosis, Useful in Certain Circumstances, last bullet added: Encourage cytology and flow cytometry of effusion fluid if present to evaluate for 
coexisting primary effusion lymphoma (PEL).
• Workup
Essential, 1st bullet, 1st sub-bullet revised: Including history of additional immunosuppressive disease or immunomodulatory therapy (eg, transplant; 
local or systemic corticosteroids), such as transplant/,local or systemic glucocorticoids.
Useful in Selected Cases
 ◊Bullet 3 added: Stool hemoccult and/or chest x-ray as clinically indicated (eg, for advanced disease).
 ◊Bullets removed:
– In the setting of advanced disease cutaneous, oral, visceral, or nodal involvement: stool hemoccult.
– In the setting of advanced cutaneous, oral, visceral, or nodal disease, or any pulmonary symptoms: chest x-ray.
 ◊6th bullet revised: If concerns for coexisting KSHV-associated inflammatory cytokine syndrome (KICS), MCD, or KSHV+ lymphoma, or other KSHV-
associated disease: CT scan or FDG-PET/CT scan and/or lab workup.
• Header revised: KS STAGE STATE (Also for KS-2 and KS-3)
KS STATE
 ◊Qualifiers removed (cutaneous, oral, visceral, or nodal) from Advanced disease pathway and added to footnote e. (Also for KS-2 and KS-3)
 ◊Pathway added: KSHV-Associated Inflammatory Cytokine Syndrome (KICS) (KICS-1)
• Footnote a added: Refer to the Oncology Guidelines Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health. See Oncology Guidelines for 
Distress Management (DIS-A).
• Footnote c revised: Imaging should be directed by symptoms or findings concerning for visceral or bone involvement as well as coexisting KICS, MCD, 
or KSHV+ lymphoma and can include whole body FDG-PET/CT. See Oncology Guidelines for B-Cell Lymphomas (CD-1) Oncology Guidelines for Castleman 
Disease. (Also for KS-4)
• Footnote e added: Advanced disease refers to disease that is not limited to cutaneous disease (eg, advanced cutaneous, oral, visceral, or nodal 
disease). See Staging and Classification for KS (KS-A 1 of 2) for definitions of T0 and T1. (Also for KS-2 and KS-3)
• Footnote f added: Patients with KICS present with symptoms such as fever, fatigue, edema, cachexia, gastrointestinal or respiratory symptoms, pain, 
neuropathy, and/or an altered mental state.
KS-2
• Footnote r added: See Response Definitions for KS (KS-A 2 of 2). (Also for KS-3)
UPDATES
Continued

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
UPDATES
KS-4
• Bullet 1 added: Patients should be educated on the signs of disease progression and encouraged to promptly report any concerns.
• 2nd bullet, 1st sub-bullet revised: Periodic, life-long follow-up should be patient-centered with timing based on response to therapy and, if applicable, 
presence of immunosuppressive therapy or condition including degree of HIV viremia and immune reconstitution.
• Sub-sub-bullet 1 added: Dermatology, medical oncology, hematology, and primary HIV care should be included as appropriate.
• Bullet 4, sub-bullet 2 added: Stool hemoccult and/or chest x-ray as clinically indicated (eg, for advanced disease).
• Bullet 4, sub-sub bullet 1 added: When effusions are present, encourage cytology and flow cytometry to evaluate for PEL.
• Bullet 4, sub-bullet removed: In the setting of advanced cutaneous, oral, visceral, or nodal involvement: stool hemoccult.
• Bullet 4, sub-bullet removed: In the setting of advanced cutaneous, oral, visceral, or nodal disease: chest x-ray.
• Footnote v added: For additional considerations in the post-treatment setting, see the Oncology Guidelines for Survivorship.
KICS-1
• New Algorithm: KSHV-Associated Inflammatory Cytokine Syndrome (KICS)
KS-A (2 of 2)
• Footnote b added: Although this response criteria was developed for HIV-associated KS, it is applied to all forms of KS in clinical trials.
KS-B
• Principles of Therapy
2nd bullet revised: Optimization of immune function and avoidance of additional immunosuppression are critical to prevention of additional KS lesions 
and maintenance of response to therapy. For HIV-related KS, reconstitution of immune function and maintenance of viral suppression are important. 
Also, it is important to work with an HIV specialist to optimize suppression of HIV and reconstitution of immune function with ART. In patients with KS 
who require immunosuppression, collaboration with a pharmacist to discuss treatment options is recommended.
• Goals of Therapy
2nd bullet revised: Patients with symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome disease should use minimally invasive and the least toxic therapy to control 
disease (ie, local therapy, RT, limited systemic therapy). When necessary, a limited number of cycles of systemic therapy (eg, 3–6) may be sufficient 
for those PWH initiating or re-initiating ART.
3rd bullet revised: Patients with advanced symptomatic cutaneous, visceral, nodal, or oral disease should be treated with systemic therapy with the 
goal of reducing or reversing symptoms, lymphedema, or threat to organ function. Complete remissions are rare.
KS-C
• Management
2nd bullet, 1st sub-bullet revised: Consider thalidomide Corticosteroid-sparing treatment such as thalidomide can be considered as an active agent 
against both KS and corticosteroid-refractory IRIS.
Updates in Version 1.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Kaposi Sarcoma from Version 2.2025 include:
Continued

Table of Contents
Discussion
UPDATES
KS-F (1 of 4)
• First-line systemic therapy options revised:
For non-transplant KS
 ◊Preferred
– Liposomal Doxorubicin
 ◊Other recommended
– Paclitaxel
For transplant KS
 ◊Preferred
– Sirolimus
 ◊Other recommended
– Liposomal Doxorubicin
– Paclitaxel
• Subsequent systemic therapy options for relapsed/refractory therapy revised (Also for KS-F 2):
Nivolumab and Pembrolizumab changed from useful in certain circumstances.
• Useful in certain circumstances revised:
Bortezomib, Gemcitabine, Lenalidomide, and Vinorelbine changed from preferred.
KS-F (2 of 4)
• First-Line Systemic Therapy Dosing, Other recommended regimen, sub-bullet removed: Premedication with dexamethasone may not be needed; if 
used, the dose should be minimized and tailored to patient needs.
KS-F (3 of 4)
• Footnote b added: Corticosteroid pre-medication as an anti-emetic, or to prevent infusion/hypersensitivity reaction (except in cases of prior reaction) 
should generally be avoided.
• Footnote c added: An FDA-approved biosimilar is an appropriate substitute for any recommended systemic biologic therapy in the Oncology Guidelines.

• For people with HIV (PWH), begin discussions regarding the possible need to modify

e Advanced disease refers to T1 disease, extensive T0 cutaneous disease, or nodal disease. See
Staging Classification for KS (KS-A 1 of 2) for definitions of T0 and T1.
g Oncology and HIV clinicians, along with both an oncology pharmacist and HIV pharmacist, if 
available, should review proposed cancer therapy, supportive care medications, and ART for possible 
drug-drug interactions (DDIs) and overlapping toxicities prior to initiation. Co-management by an 
oncologist and an HIV clinician is recommended for the duration of therapy. See Oncology Guidelines for 
Cancer in People with HIV.
h Principles and Goals of Therapy (KS-B).
i There is no agreed upon definition for limited cutaneous disease, but some trials have used inclusion 
criteria such as 5 index lesions or <5% body surface area involvement as the threshold. Clinical 
judgment is required.
j Initiation of ART may result in immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) within 3–6 months; 
IRIS is characterized by marked lesional swelling, increased tenderness, and peripheral edema. 
Therefore, systemic KS-directed therapy should be initiated as soon as possible in all PWH who have 
symptomatic KS (eg, edema, oral KS, or other manifestation that interfere with normal functions or 
activities). In patients with limited cutaneous disease, KS-associated IRIS is an indication for urgent 
initiation of systemic KS therapy; signs of past IRIS may also be an indication for earlier initiation 
of systemic KS therapy. ART should not be delayed or discontinued unless life-threatening IRIS 
develops. Reconstitution of immune function is important for obtaining and maintaining control or 
remission of KS. See Principles of Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome (IRIS) (KS-C).

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
KS-4
SURVEILLANCEv
• Patients should be educated on the signs of disease progression and encouraged to promptly report any concerns
• For patients not requiring active therapy and with no signs of progression 
Periodic, life-long follow-up should be patient-centered with timing based on response to therapy and, if applicable, presence of 
immunosuppressive therapy or condition including degree of HIV viremia and immune reconstitution
 ◊Dermatology, medical oncology, hematology, and primary HIV care should be included as appropriate
 ◊Follow-up should include the following:
– History and physical examination
▪Including history of additional immunosuppression such as transplant/glucocorticoids 
	▪Including complete skin and oral examinations, and evaluation and monitoring of edema
	
– CBC, differential, comprehensive metabolic panel
– PWH
▪T-cell subsets (CD4+ T-cell count) and HIV viral load
	▪Assess ART compliance
• Photography of oral, conjunctival, and cutaneous lesions (with reference unit of measure in the picture) for evaluation of extent of disease 
if change in disease is noted
• If signs and symptoms concerning for visceral involvement or prior to new therapy if progression/refractory disease or if change in disease 
is noted, the following are indicated depending on the clinical circumstancesc:
Evaluation for suspected OIs
Stool hemoccult and/or chest x-ray as clinically indicated (eg, for advanced disease)
If unexplained pulmonary symptoms or abnormalities on chest x-ray: chest CT with contrastc and bronchoscopy 
If GI symptoms or positive hemoccult: abdomen/pelvis CTc with contrast or MRI with and without contrastc and EGD/colonoscopy 
If concerns for coexisting KICS, MCD, or KSHV+ lymphoma, or other KSHV-associated disease: FDG-PET/CTc and/or lab workupd
 ◊When effusions are present, encourage cytology and flow cytometry to evaluate for PEL.
The diagnosis of KICS ideally requires excisional biopsy of lymphadenopathy to exclude MCD, if feasible
• As KSHV is not eradicated with treatment of KS, the risk for future KS persists even after complete remission.
• For PWH, optimization and monitoring of HIV control and immune function is important to minimize this risk. This risk depends on immune 
function and generally decreases with immune reconstitution. However, KS can persist, relapse, or present even in the setting of normal 
values of T-cell subsets. Less frequent (every 6–12 mo) oncology monitoring may be appropriate for selected patients with undetectable HIV 
viral loads, normal T-cell subsets, and stable KS for 2 or more years as long as the patient has regular follow-up with an HIV provider.
c Imaging should be directed by symptoms or findings concerning for visceral or bone involvement as well as coexisting KICS, MCD, or KSHV+ lymphoma and can 
include whole body FDG-PET/CT. See Oncology Guidelines for Castleman Disease.
d Useful in patients with clinical features (ie, fever, dyspnea, effusions) concerning for KICS or KSHV-associated MCD: C-reactive protein, KSHV serum viral load, 
SPEP, IL-6, or IL-10.
v For additional considerations in the post-treatment setting, see Oncology Guidelines for Survivorship.

Complete response 
(CR)
The absence of any detectable residual disease, including tumor-associated (local) edema, persisting 
for at least 4 weeks. Patients known to have had visceral disease should have restaging with 
appropriate endoscopic or radiographic procedures relevant to sites involved at baseline.
Partial response 
(PR)
No new mucocutaneous lesions, visceral sites of involvement, or the appearance or worsening of 
tumor-associated edema or effusions; AND
A 50% or greater decrease in the number of all previous existing lesions lasting for at least 4 weeks; 
OR
Complete flattening of at least 50% of all previously raised lesions (ie, 50% of all previously nodular 
or plaque-like lesions become macules); 
     OR
A 50% decrease in the sum of the products of the largest perpendicular diameters of at least 5 
measurable lesions.
NOTE: When there is residual tumor-associated edema or effusion, but disease otherwise meets criteria 
for complete response, response should be classified as "partial."
Stable disease (SD)
Any response that does not meet the criteria for progressive disease or PR.
Progressive disease 
(PD)
An increase of >25% in the size of pre-existing lesions and/or the appearance of new lesions or sites 
of disease and/or a change in the character of the skin or oral lesions from macular to plaque-like 
or nodular of >25%. If new or increasing tumor-associated edema or effusion develop, disease is 
considered to be progressive.

KS-B
PRINCIPLES AND GOALS OF THERAPY
Principles of Therapy:
• Individual KS lesions may be distinct clones that arise due to the common risk factors of immunosuppression and persistent KSHV infection 
as opposed to metastases. Treatment of existing disease therefore may not prevent occurrence of future lesions. 
• Optimization of immune function and avoidance of additional immunosuppression are critical to prevention of additional KS lesions and 
maintenance of response to therapy. For HIV-related KS, reconstitution of immune function and maintenance of viral suppression are 
important. Also, it is important to work with an HIV specialist to optimize suppression of HIV and reconstitution of immune function with ART. 
In patients with KS who require immunosuppression, collaboration with a pharmacist to discuss treatment options is recommended. 
Important examples of iatrogenic immunosuppression, which may promote KS, include not only systemic but local glucocorticoids (ie, 
inhaled, topical, intra-articular). Note that KS may flare in a remote location from the site of local glucocorticoids. 
 ◊Glucocorticoids in any formulation should be avoided due to their association with KS progression.1-3 However, in cases of life-
threatening conditions, their use may be considered. Proceed with caution if using glucocorticoids or consult an HIV or KS specialist.
Patients requiring rituximab for treatment of non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL) with coexisting KS or MCD may develop flares of KS or incident 
KS. This may be mitigated by use of concurrent chemotherapy active against both KS and disease for which rituximab is prescribed (ie, 
doxorubicin).
• Persons with HIV-related KS, especially those with advanced immunosuppression, are at increased risk of OIs, marrow suppression with 
neutropenic fever, or thrombocytopenic bleeding and should be monitored closely. It is important to collaborate with an HIV specialist to 
ensure adequate OI prophylaxis appropriate to CD4+ T-cell count (which may temporarily decrease with cytotoxic chemotherapy). Growth 
factor support may be needed to facilitate systemic therapy. 
• Lymphedema and soft tissue infections: KS is often complicated by lymphedema with increased risk of cellulitis and deep tissue infections 
in affected limbs. Risk of severe lymphedema and delayed wound healing may be increased after radiation. Refer to a lymphedema specialist. 
In the setting of advanced cutaneous disease, radiation should be reserved for circumstances when systemic therapy is not feasible with the 
goal of palliation or short-term disease management until systemic therapy may be delivered. Note that treatment responses may be delayed 
in the context of significant lymphedema.
Goals of Therapy:
• PWH with limited cutaneous disease that is asymptomatic and cosmetically acceptable may be observed while starting or continuing 
ART with optimization of immune function and HIV viral suppression as above. Remissions or stable disease may occur with ART and 
optimization of immune function and HIV viral suppression alone.
• Patients with symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome disease should use minimally invasive and the least toxic therapy to control disease 
(ie, local therapy, RT, limited systemic therapy). When necessary, a limited number of cycles of systemic therapy (eg, 3–6) may be sufficient 
for PWH initiating or re-initiating ART.
• Patients with advanced symptomatic disease should be treated with systemic therapy with the goal of reducing or reversing symptoms, 
lymphedema, or threat to organ function. Complete remissions are rare. 
Treatment is typically continued until unacceptable toxicity or plateau in response; maintenance therapy beyond 2 cycles of systemic 
therapy after determination of plateau is not recommended. If response is then clinically acceptable, patients may be observed (while 
continuing ART, in PWH). Otherwise, alternative therapy should be initiated.

3 Volkow PF, Cornejo P, Zinser JW, et al. Life-threatening exacerbation of Kaposi's sarcoma after prednisone treatment for immune reconstitution inflammatory 
syndrome. AIDS 2008;22:663-665.

KS-C

header: PRINCIPLES OF IMMUNE RECONSTITUTION INFLAMMATORY SYNDROME (IRIS)
content:
CLINICAL PRESENTATION/DEFINITION1
• IRIS is characterized by first presentation or paradoxical worsening of pre-existing KS infection following ART initiation.
• Typically occurs within 3 months of ART initiation, but possible to occur any time after starting ART.
• PWH with advanced KS not on ART at the time of presentation, should start systemic therapy as soon as possible after the initiation of ART 
to minimize the risk of severe KS-IRIS.
• The risk of IRIS is higher in patients with baseline low CD4 T-cell counts and high HIV viral loads.2
• Clinical manifestations may include development of new lesions or enlargement of existing lesions, worsening lymphadenopathy, or 
worsening edema. The clinical presentation of IRIS may be challenging to distinguish from the natural history of progressive disease.
• If IRIS is suspected, consult with an HIV specialist.
MANAGEMENT
• Treatment for KS-IRIS includes systemic chemotherapy and supportive measures; ART should not be discontinued.
• Avoid corticosteroids, which may exacerbate KS.
• If the patient is on ART only: Symptomatic management; consider addition of systemic chemotherapy (see list of first-line options on KS-F).
Corticosteroid-sparing treatment such as thalidomide can be considered as an active agent against both KS and IRIS.
• If the patient is on ART and chemotherapy: Symptomatic management; consider modification of chemotherapy regimen if progressive.
• Use of systemic chemotherapy for extensive disease prior to ART initiation may help prevent KS-IRIS, but this has not been systematically 
studied.
PROPOSED CRITERIA FOR DIAGNOSIS OF KS-IRIS3
KS-IRIS requires at least one major and one minor criterion:
Major Criteria
• New onset or enlargement of KS lesion and subsequent regression
• Painful lesions
Minor Criteria
• Decrease in plasma HIV RNA by >1 log10 copies/mL
• Increased blood CD4 T-cell count after ART
1 Letang E, Lewis JJ, Bower M, et al. Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome associated with Kaposi sarcoma: higher incidence and mortality in Africa than in 
the UK. AIDS 2013;27:1603-1613.
2 Panel on Antiretroviral Guidelines for Adults and Adolescents. Guidelines for the Use of Antiretroviral Agents in Adults and Adolescents with HIV. Department of 
Health and Human Services. Available at https://clinicalinfo.hiv.gov/en/guidelines/adult-and-adolescent-arv. Accessed September 6, 2022.
3 French MA, Price P, Stone SF. Immune restoration disease after antiretroviral therapy. AIDS 2004;18:1615-1627.

• General Principles
For most skin lesions, electrons or superficial x-rays can be used to deliver optimal dosimetry and minimize dose to underlying structures. 
To ensure sufficient dose is delivered for deeper or larger lesions, conformal photon therapy or mixed photon-electron treatment plans 
may be used. Intensity-modulated RT (IMRT) with or without image guidance may be useful for larger, deeper disease, or disease located 
anatomically adjacent to critical structures.  
The use of bolus may be necessary to achieve adequate skin dose.
RT to plantar and palmar surfaces might be useful in selected cases. However, high doses should be approached with caution because of 
theoretical toxicity concerns like long-term wound healing, particularly in the setting of coexisting lymphedema. 
Risk of secondary cancer, severe or worsening lymphedema, and long-term wound healing complications may be increased after radiation; 
however, toxicity can be mitigated by utilizing lower-dose radiotherapy regimens.  
Caution should be exercised with the use of RT to sites of pre-existing lymphedema. In the setting of advanced cutaneous disease, 
radiation should be reserved for circumstances when systemic therapy is not feasible with the goal of palliation or short-term disease 
management until systemic therapy may be delivered.
• General Treatment Information 
Dosing Prescription Regimen1,2,3
 ◊Various dosing schemas may be used. Lower total doses are preferred for smaller and more superficial lesions and palliative situations. 
Higher doses may be preferred for more extensive, deeply invasive lesions, or when a more durable local response is desired. More 
fractionated regimens may be preferred for sites with adjacent radiosensitive structures, such as the oral cavity. Examples:

Oncology Guidelines Version 2.2026 
Kaposi Sarcoma
MS-2
Overview
Kaposi sarcoma is a multifocal malignancy of endothelial cells, which 
presents with characteristic purpuric, red, or brown macules, papules, and 
plaques. Four types of Kaposi sarcoma have been described: HIV-
associated, classic, iatrogenic or transplant-associated, and endemic.1-3 
Kaposi sarcoma is universally associated with Kaposi sarcoma-associated 
herpesvirus (KSHV) infection (also known as human herpesvirus-8, or 
HHV-8).2 Serologic confirmation of KSHV infection is present in 95% to 
98% of patients with Kaposi sarcoma.2,3 KSHV infections are usually 
asymptomatic. Like other herpesviruses, KSHV establishes latent infection 
in humans that is lifelong. Hence, persons with KSHV infection are at 
lifelong risk of Kaposi sarcoma and other KSHV-associated diseases, 
including KSHV-inflammatory cytokine syndrome (KICS), KSHV-
associated multicentric Castleman disease (MCD) and primary effusion 
lymphoma (PEL). Immunosuppression of various forms (ie, 
immunosenescence, human immunodeficiency virus [HIV]-associated and 
iatrogenic) is recognized to be an important factor in the pathogenesis of 
Kaposi sarcoma.
The Oncology Guidelines Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology (Oncology Guidelines®) 
for Kaposi Sarcoma provide treatment recommendations for patients with 
HIV-associated, classic, iatrogenic, and endemic Kaposi sarcoma and are 
intended to assist health care providers with clinical decision-making. This 
Discussion section provides an overview of the literature supporting the 
recommendations included in the guidelines. The Panel also publishes 
separate Oncology Guidelines for Cancer in People with HIV (available at 
www.Oncology Guidelines), which give recommendations regarding HIV 
management during cancer therapy, drug-drug interactions (DDIs), 
radiation therapy, and supportive care for people with HIV (PWH) who 
have cancer. Recommendations for the management of Kaposi sarcoma 
for patients in Sub-Saharan Africa can be found in the International 
Adaption of the Oncology Guidelines for Sub-Saharan Africa for Kaposi
Sarcoma (available at https://www.Oncology Guidelines/global/what-we-
do/international-adaptations).
Guidelines Update Methodology
The complete details of the Development and Update of the Oncology Guidelines 
Guidelines are available on the Oncology Guidelines website at www.Oncology Guidelines.
Literature Search Criteria
Prior to the update of the Oncology Guidelines® for Kaposi Sarcoma, an 
electronic search of the PubMed database was performed to obtain key 
literature in Kaposi Sarcoma published since the previous Guidelines 
update, using the search term: Kaposi Sarcoma. The PubMed database 
was chosen because it remains the most widely used resource for medical 
literature and indexes peer-reviewed biomedical literature.4
The search results were narrowed by selecting studies in humans 
published in English. Results were confined to the following article types: 
Case Reports; Clinica Study; Clinical Trial, Phase II; Clinical Trial, Phase 
III; Clinical Trial, Phase IV; Controlled Clinical Trial; Evaluation Study; 
Guideline; Multicenter Study; Observational Study; Practice Guideline; 
Randomized Controlled Trial; Meta-Analysis; Systematic Reviews; and 
Validation Studies.
The data from key PubMed articles as well as articles from additional 
sources deemed as relevant to these guidelines as discussed by the 
Panel during the Guidelines update have been included in this version of 
the Discussion section. Recommendations for which high-level evidence is 
lacking are based on the Panel’s review of lower-level evidence and 
expert opinion.

Sensitive/Inclusive Language Usage
Oncology Guidelines strive to use language that advances the goals of 
equity, inclusion, and representation.5 Oncology Guidelines endeavor to use 
language that is person-first; not stigmatizing; anti-racist, anti-classist, 
anti-misogynist, anti-ageist, anti-ableist, and anti-weight-biased; and 
inclusive of individuals of all sexual orientations and gender identities. 
Oncology Guidelines incorporate non-gendered language, instead focusing 
on organ-specific recommendations. This language is both more accurate 
and more inclusive and can help fully address the needs of individuals of 
all sexual orientations and gender identities. Oncology Guidelines will 
continue to use the terms men, women, female, and male when citing 
statistics, recommendations, or data from organizations or sources that do 
not use inclusive terms. Most studies do not report how sex and gender 
data are collected and use these terms interchangeably or inconsistently. 
If sources do not differentiate gender from sex assigned at birth or organs 
present, the information is presumed to predominantly represent cisgender 
individuals. Oncology Guidelines encourages researchers to collect more specific data in 
future studies and organizations to use more inclusive and accurate 
language in their future analyses.
HIV-Associated Kaposi Sarcoma
Historically, when Kaposi sarcoma occurred in the setting of HIV 
seropositivity, it was considered an AIDS-defining illness and was referred 
to as AIDS-related or epidemic Kaposi sarcoma. The risk for Kaposi 
sarcoma in the setting of HIV was previously reported to be increased as 
much as 3640-fold over the general U.S. population,6-10 but this risk has 
declined in the antiretroviral therapy (ART) era.6,11-14 Still, estimates 
indicate that the risk of Kaposi sarcoma in PWH between 2000 and 2015 
was elevated approximately 498- to 512-fold compared with the general 
U.S. population,11,15 and Kaposi sarcoma accounted for approximately 7% 
of cancers diagnosed in PWH, with an estimated 540 cases diagnosed in
the United States in 2020.16 Five-year survival of patients with HIV-related 
Kaposi sarcoma has improved in the post-ART era, from 12.1% in 1980 to 
1995 to as high as 88% in the post-ART era.17-19
When immunosuppression is advanced, HIV-associated Kaposi sarcoma 
is more common, more aggressive, and more likely to involve viscera, 
mucosa, and lymph nodes and to cause lymphedema than when 
immunosuppression is minimal. In fact, CD4+ T-cell counts and HIV viral 
load correlate with the risk of Kaposi sarcoma development in PWH, and 
effective ART lowers that risk.20 Evidence also suggests that ART 
improves prognosis of Kaposi sarcoma in PWH.
HIV-associated Kaposi sarcoma can also occur in PWH with normal CD4+ 
T-cell counts and undetectable HIV viral load. Like classic Kaposi 
sarcoma, these cases commonly occur with advanced age. Even with 
well-controlled HIV, studies have demonstrated more rapid 
immunosenescence in PWH than people without HIV, which may permit 
Kaposi sarcoma to become clinically apparent in KSHV-seropositive PWH. 
Typically presenting with more limited disease than in PWH with advanced 
immunosuppression, these cases have prompted a shift toward favoring 
the term “HIV-associated Kaposi sarcoma” over “AIDS-related Kaposi 
sarcoma” considering its occurrence in PWH with otherwise preserved 
immune function as well as in those with advanced immunosuppression.
HIV management during treatment of Kaposi sarcoma in PWH is critical. 
Co-management by oncology and HIV specialists is recommended for the 
duration of therapy. Oncology and HIV specialists, along with both an 
oncology pharmacist and HIV pharmacist if available, should review 
proposed cancer therapy, supportive care medications, and ART for 
possible DDIs and overlapping toxicities prior to initiation of therapy for 
PWH. The Oncology Guidelines for Cancer in People with HIV (available at 
www.Oncology Guidelines) provide additional recommendations on HIV screening,

Classic Kaposi Sarcoma
Classic Kaposi sarcoma generally involves indolent cutaneous lesions, 
often of the lower extremities, which may wax and wane or slowly 
progress over years to decades. It is most common in people of 
Mediterranean, Eastern European, Middle Eastern, and/or Jewish origins, 
with a mean age of 74 years at diagnosis.1,3 It is reported to be 7 to 15 
times more common in males than in females.1,3 Synchronous primary 
malignancies may be fairly common in patients with classic Kaposi 
sarcoma, and are more likely to be the cause of death in these patients.3
Iatrogenic Kaposi Sarcoma
When Kaposi sarcoma occurs in the context of immunosuppressive 
therapy (for organ transplant or other reasons), it is called iatrogenic or 
transplant-associated Kaposi sarcoma.1 Lesions often appear 2 to 8 
months after initiation of immunosuppression, and occur 2 to 3 times more 
often in males.1 Although this form of Kaposi sarcoma can be aggressive 
and involve lymph nodes, mucosa, and/or visceral organs, it frequently 
responds to cessation of immunosuppression. When this is not feasible, 
reduction of immunosuppression or change to use of mTOR inhibitors as 
the immunosuppressive therapy can improve control of Kaposi sarcoma.21 
More information on iatrogenic Kaposi sarcoma is included below (see 
Optimizing Immune Function).
Endemic Kaposi Sarcoma
Endemic Kaposi sarcoma occurs in children and younger adults (<40 
years of age) of equatorial Africa and may also occur in immigrants from 
the region. It is usually more aggressive than classic Kaposi sarcoma, 
sometimes with visceral, bone, and/or lymph node involvement, although it 
often begins as skin lesions that remain indolent for several years.1 As
with classic Kaposi sarcoma, endemic disease is 10 to 17 times more 
common in males than in females.1
Other KSHV-associated conditions
Additional KSHV-associated conditions may arise synchronously or 
asynchronously with Kaposi sarcoma, including PEL, MCD, and KICS.22-24 
As each of these conditions typically follow an aggressive clinical course 
requiring management beyond that indicated for Kaposi sarcoma alone, it 
is important that providers caring for persons with Kaposi sarcoma are 
aware of the presenting features and management considerations for 
these additional KSHV-associated conditions, as discussed below.
Primary Effusion Lymphoma
PEL is a rare, aggressive CD-20-negative B-cell lymphoma associated 
with immunosuppression, particularly in the setting of advanced HIV 
infection. It presents with malignant effusions of clonal CD38+, KSHV+ B-
cells, with or without lymphadenopathy. Among PWH, a history of Kaposi 
sarcoma is associated with 59-fold higher PEL incidence compared with 
those without a history of any diagnosis historically defined as AIDS-
defining.25 Poor performance status, advanced stage disease, and lack of 
ART treatment of HIV prior to the diagnosis of PEL have been identified as 
prognostic factors for shorter survival. A PEL-PS model was recently 
proposed, finding that the combination of ECOG performance status >2 
and hemoglobin <8 g/dL was associated with poor survival rates in HIV-
associated PEL.26 Cytology and flow cytometry of effusion fluid can be 
used to evaluate for coexisting PEL in patients with Kaposi sarcoma. More 
information on PEL can be found in the Oncology Guidelines for B-Cell 
Lymphomas, under HIV-Related B-Cell Lymphomas (available at 
www.Oncology Guidelines).

Kaposi Sarcoma
MS-5
KSVH-Associated MCD and KICS
Unexplained fevers occurring in the context of Kaposi sarcoma, 
particularly when associated with lymphadenopathy/splenomegaly and 
cytopenias, should prompt workup of MCD and KICS, with imaging, 
C-reactive protein, KSHV serum viral load, serum protein electrophoresis 
(SPEP), IL-6, and IL-10.22-24
KSHV-Associated MCD 
MCD is classified as tumor-like lesion with B-cell predominance, which 
may be idiopathic or associated with KSHV.27 KSHV-associated MCD is a 
remitting-relapsing disease that typically follows an aggressive course. 
Immunocompromising conditions are a primary risk factor for KSHV-
associated MCD, with HIV being the most common.28 Clinical presentation 
includes multifocal lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, signs of fluid 
accumulation (edema/effusions), cytopenias, liver and kidney dysfunction, 
and constitutional symptoms (fevers, night sweats) driven by cytokines 
such as IL-6.29-31 KSHV viremia is typically marked during flares of MCD 
(≥4 log) and undetectable or low level during remissions (~3 log).31 More 
information on MCD is available in the Oncology Guidelines for Castleman 
Disease (available at www.Oncology Guidelines).
KICS 
KICS and KSHV-associated MCD share clinical features of an 
inflammatory syndrome with often dramatic KSHV viremia.22-24 KICS is a 
diagnosis of exclusion, distinguished from MCD and lymphoma by 
absence of pathologic characteristics of either upon biopsy of 
lymphadenopathy or other masses.22 KICS may also mimic Kaposi 
sarcoma-associated immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (KS-
IRIS), but is distinguished from it by not being temporally tied with initiation 
of ART as expected with IRIS.
The working definition of KICS proposed by Polizotto et al involves the 
presence of specific clinical symptoms, evidence of inflammation and 
KSHV viremia, and the exclusion of MCD when lymphadenopathy is 
present (see Table 1).22 Lymphadenopathy in KICS is often less marked 
than can be seen with MCD, and there are no specific histologic features 
currently associated with KICS.
Optimal management of KICS is under investigation, with current 
standards paralleling that of KSHV-associated MCD. Liposomal 
Doxorubicin as Kaposi sarcoma-directed therapy with Rituximab has 
resulted in control of KICS.32 This regimen is therefore the preferred option 
for patients with KICS. Other Kaposi sarcoma-directed therapy with 
Rituximab may also be considered. In mild cases, Kaposi sarcoma-
directed therapy alone may be sufficient, and Rituximab may be added 
later if disease response is inadequate. For transplant recipients, the 
Sirolimus component of immunosuppression should be maximized and 
other components should be minimized. Early consultation with Kaposi 
sarcoma experts is encouraged.
If inflammatory syndrome and lymphadenopathy/organomegaly improve, 
Rituximab can be withheld while Kaposi sarcoma-directed therapy is 
continued for 3 months or for as long as needed for adequate/optimal 
Kaposi sarcoma response. Rituximab should be resumed if inflammatory 
symptoms or lab markers flare after Rituximab is stopped.
For refractory cases, management strategies like that used for KSHV-
MCD may be effective. Thus, the addition of anti-IL-6 directed therapy (ie, 
Tocilizumab or Siltuximab) to Kaposi sarcoma-directed therapy should be 
considered.33-35

MS-6
Management of Kaposi Sarcoma

header: Diagnosis and Workup of Kaposi Sarcoma
content:
Multiple clinical and histologic presentations of Kaposi sarcoma have been 
described. Mucosal and cutaneous lesions may be characterized clinically 
as papules, plaques, nodules (sometime pedunculated), and bullae. Large 
plaques may form from coalescence of smaller plaques or nodules and 
may ulcerate or develop bullae. Hyperpigmented macules (lacking 
palpable change in skin thickness) rarely represent active disease; rather, 
they are very common after lesion regression due to residual hemosiderin-
related pigmentation. Histologic subtypes include anaplastic, 
telangiectatic, lymphedematous, hyperkeratotic, keloidal, micronodular, 
pyogenic granuloma-like, ecchymotic, and intravascular variants of Kaposi 
sarcoma.
Lymphedema is a common complication of Kaposi sarcoma and may also 
be a predisposing factor to the development of Kaposi sarcoma. 
Lymphedema can be caused by not only nodal involvement but 
involvement of lymphatic vessels. Hyperkeratotic variants with verrucous 
and hyperkeratotic changes are notably associated with chronic and 
severe Kaposi sarcoma-associated lymphedema and may require deeper 
biopsy to confirm the presence of Kaposi sarcoma.
As described in the guidelines above, Kaposi sarcoma is diagnosed by 
pathology and immunophenotyping. Workup should include evaluation for 
history of immunosuppressive condition or therapy, such as transplant, 
local or systemic glucocorticoids (including topical, intranasal, inhaled, 
intraarticular, as well as systemic), or other immunosuppressive therapies. 
Complete skin, oral, and lymph node exams, including evaluation of 
edema and photography of oral, conjunctival, and cutaneous lesions for 
evaluation and monitoring of extent of disease are recommended. It is 
important to note that certain opportunistic infections can result in 
cutaneous lesions that can mimic Kaposi sarcoma lesions (eg, bacillary
angiomatosis, blastomycosis, cryptococcosis).36-39 Therefore, in addition to 
biopsy of suspected lesions, involvement of an HIV specialist to evaluate 
for coexisting opportunistic infections is appropriate in PWH and those 
with advanced immunosuppression. Laboratory evaluation should include 
complete blood count (CBC) with differential, comprehensive metabolic 
panel, HIV screening, and/or diagnostic testing.
For PWH, care coordination between the HIV specialist and the oncology 
team is recommended. Recommendations for the management of cancer 
in PWH, including the possible need to modify ART due to DDIs, are 
presented in the Oncology Guidelines for Cancer in People with HIV 
(available at www.Oncology Guidelines). All PWH should have recent T-cell subsets 
including quantitative CD4+ T-cell counts and HIV viral load to assess 
immune function and HIV control.
Depending on symptoms and findings that may be concerning for visceral 
or bone involvement, additional workup may be necessary. This may 
include fecal occult blood testing (FOBT), chest x-ray, 
chest/abdomen/pelvis CTs with contrast, MRI with and without contrast, 
bronchoscopy, upper endoscopy, or colonoscopy.
In the event of suspected coexisting KSHV-associated PEL, MCD, or 
KICS, additional workup is necessary, including imaging, such as 
fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG)-PET/CT scans, the laboratory workup noted 
above, cytology with flow cytometry of effusions, and biopsy of 
lymphadenopathy (excisional biopsy is optimal).
In persons with HIV-associated Kaposi sarcoma, it is important to note that 
interpretation of imaging in PWH who have cancer is complicated by the 
increased incidence of non-malignant lesions that may be mistaken for 
cancer spread or recurrence. Opportunistic infections in the lung include 
Mycobacterium tuberculosis (TB), cytomegalovirus (CMV), invasive fungal 
infections, and Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP).40 Furthermore,

MS-7
non-infectious, non-malignant pulmonary manifestations of HIV can be 
difficult to interpret on imaging studies, including interstitial pneumonia and 
granulomatous disease.40,41 Additionally, brain lesions seen in PWH may 
result from opportunistic infections, such as viral encephalitis, 
aspergillosis, toxoplasmosis, cryptococcosis, bacterial meningitis, 
tuberculosis, progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy, and 
mycobacterium avium complex (MAC).42,43 PWH are at increased risk of 
lymphomas including primary central nervous system lymphoma. Benign 
non-infectious brain lesions can also occur in PWH (eg, vascular 
complications, hydrocephalus).42,43 Similarly, immune response to HIV and 
opportunistic infections commonly cause lymphadenopathy in PWH, which 
can be seen on CT and may be avid on F-18 FDG PET/CT.44,45 
Non-malignant causes of lymphadenopathy are more marked in patients 
with higher HIV viral loads and lower CD4+ T-cell counts.46 Therefore, 
involvement of an HIV specialist to assist with determining the need for a 
workup of suspected concurrent opportunistic infection or HIV-associated 
complications may be appropriate.

As delineated in the guidelines above, Kaposi sarcoma is staged using a 
TIS system in which aspects of the tumor (T), immune system (I), and 
systemic disease (S) are assessed with a 0 for good risk and 1 for poor 
risk.47 Data have shown that the I stage has less prognostic value than the 
T or S stages for PWH on ART.18 Kaposi sarcoma staged as T1S1 
appears to have the worst prognosis. In a study of 211 patients with 
HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma, those staged as T1S1 had a 3-year survival 
rate of 53%, whereas for those staged as T0S0, T1S0, or T0S1, the 3-year 
survival rates were 88%, 80%, and 81%, respectively (P = .0001).18 
Notably, Kaposi sarcoma management, as outlined in these guidelines, is 
primarily stratified by limited cutaneous versus advanced disease (ie, T1 
disease, extensive T0 cutaneous disease, or nodal disease), reflecting the 
tumor (T) component of the TIS staging system.
Assessing Response of Kaposi Sarcoma
Response of Kaposi sarcoma to therapy has been formally defined by the 
AIDS Clinical Trials Group (ACTG) Oncology Committee as follows47:
• 
Complete response (CR) is defined as the absence of any 
detectable residual disease, including tumor-associated (local) 
edema, persisting for at least 4 weeks. Patients known to have had 
visceral disease should have restaging with appropriate 
endoscopic or radiographic procedures relevant to sites involved at 
baseline. 
• 
Partial response (PR) is defined as no new mucocutaneous 
lesions, visceral sites of involvement, or the appearance or 
worsening of tumor-associated edema or effusions; AND
o A ≥50% decrease in the number of all previously existing
lesions lasting for at least 4 weeks; OR 
o Complete flattening of at least 50% of all previously raised
lesions (ie, 50% of all previously nodular or plaque-like 
lesion become macules); OR 
o A 50% decrease in the sum of the products of the largest
perpendicular diameters of at least five measurable lesions. 
o Note that when there is residual tumor-associated edema
or effusion, but disease otherwise meets criteria for CR, 
response should be classified as partial. 
• 
Stable disease (SD) is defined as any response that does not meet 
the criteria for progressive disease (PD) or PR. 
• 
PD is defined as an increase of ≥25% in the size of pre-existing 
lesions and/or the appearance of new lesions or sites of disease 
and/or a change in the character of the skin or oral lesions from 
macular to plaque-like or nodular of ≥25%. If new or increasing 
tumor-associated edema or effusion develop, disease is 
considered progressive.

MS-8
Many Kaposi sarcoma lesions that are responsive to therapy will flatten 
and change color but remain pigmented (ranging from very dark brown to 
tan) as non-palpable macular skin lesions. Biopsy of these will often 
identify residual siderophages and/or free hemosiderin pigment in the 
tissue without tumor cells. This is attributed to long-term iron deposition 
resulting from red blood cell extravasation into the tissue (dermal layer 
characteristically in cutaneous lesions). Care should be taken to 
distinguish this “tattoo” effect from active disease, as additional therapy is 
not indicated for the former. Over time, many lesions will gradually fade, 
depending on many factors, including chronicity and size of the lesion as 
well as degree to which red blood cell extravasation occurred.
Optimizing Immune Function
For all types and severities of Kaposi sarcoma, optimizing immune 
function is a critical component of management, and in some cases may 
be the only intervention needed for initial control. In PWH, maximizing HIV 
suppression and immune reconstitution with appropriate ART is therefore 
critical.
Iatrogenic immunosuppression—including over-the-counter topical or 
intranasal, as well as prescription inhaled, locally injected or systemic 
glucocorticoids—is often identified on a careful medication history in 
persons with and without HIV prior to initial presentation or flare of Kaposi 
sarcoma. Rituximab is also associated with initial presentation or flare of 
Kaposi sarcoma in individuals without HIV, related to the associated 
activation of KSHV.48-51 Patients and their providers should be educated 
on the importance of cessation and avoidance of immunosuppressive 
therapies whenever possible, including topical, local, and systemic 
therapies. Otherwise, immunosuppressive therapy should be minimized as 
much as possible to reduce risk of Kaposi sarcoma progression and 
flares.52-54
Sirolimus is an mTOR inhibitor that functions as an immunosuppressant. 
mTOR inhibition suppresses Kaposi sarcoma tumors, so–unlike other 
immunosuppressants–Sirolimus can be an effective treatment against 
transplant-associated Kaposi sarcoma.21 Furthermore, when Sirolimus is 
administered in patients with transplant Kaposi sarcoma, glucocorticoids 
and other immunosuppressive agents can often be halted or minimized. 
Sirolimus is discussed in more detail below (see First-Line Systemic 
Therapy).
Limited Cutaneous Kaposi Sarcoma
PWH with limited cutaneous disease that is asymptomatic and 
cosmetically acceptable to the patient may be treated with ART alone (see 
below). Persons without HIV can be observed until the disease becomes 
symptomatic (eg, edema, oral Kaposi Sarcoma, or other manifestation that 
interfere with normal functions or activities) and/or cosmetically 
bothersome or progressive. Rapid progression during observation may be 
an indication for initiation of systemic Kaposi Sarcoma therapy.
PWH with symptomatic and/or cosmetically bothersome limited cutaneous 
disease should be treated with ART and with the most minimally invasive 
and least toxic therapy possible. When necessary, a limited number of 
cycles of systemic therapy (eg, 3–6; options discussed below) may be 
sufficient for those initiating or re-initiating ART. Other first-line therapy 
options include enrollment in a clinical trial or local therapy.
Local therapies include topical treatment, radiation therapy, cryotherapy, 
and for small, symptomatic lesions, marginal excision, electrodessication 
and curettage, and intralesional chemotherapy may be considered and are 
discussed in more detail below. Wide excisions with the goal of achieving 
negative margins are not indicated, as they do not contribute to control of 
Kaposi sarcoma. For larger lesions when systemic therapy is not feasible 
or effective, radiotherapy is preferred over excision. Intralesional

chemotherapy and radiation to plantar and palmar surfaces may be useful 
in selected cases but should be approached with caution because of 
toxicity or functional adverse events. Increased risk of long-term wound 
healing, particularly in the setting of coexisting lymphedema, is a concern.
Initiation of ART may result in IRIS within 3 to 6 months and patients 
should therefore be reassessed within 4 weeks for signs and symptoms 
(see Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome, below).
The same treatment options, without ART, are recommended for patients 
without HIV who have limited cutaneous disease that is symptomatic 
and/or cosmetically bothersome.
If progressive, relapsed, or refractory disease is suspected, consider 
biopsy of lesions since they may be areas of post-inflammatory pigment 
and/or mimickers of Kaposi sarcoma. If it is Kaposi sarcoma, the individual 
should be evaluated for inadequate HIV control or other 
immunosuppressant use, which could be contributing factors to 
inadequate Kaposi sarcoma control. Change in ART in conjunction with an 
HIV specialist may be warranted for some cases (see the Oncology Guidelines 
Guidelines for Cancer in People with HIV, available at www.Oncology Guidelines).
For PWH with optimized HIV control and those without HIV, Kaposi 
sarcoma-therapy should be determined by the extent of disease. If Kaposi 
sarcoma is stable or a response plateau occurs with initial therapy, the 
patient should be observed (while ART is continued in PWH). If the 
disease progresses or relapses after an initial response to therapy, repeat 
use of the previously effective therapy may be considered, particularly if 
response was durable and the therapy was well tolerated.
Advanced Cutaneous, Oral, Visceral, or Nodal Kaposi Sarcoma
First-line treatment options for patients with advanced cutaneous, oral, 
visceral, or nodal Kaposi sarcoma are systemic therapy or clinical trial
participation, in conjunction with ART for PWH. Patients not immediately 
eligible for systemic therapy should undergo medical optimization to 
address reversible issues (eg, active infection requiring antibiotic or 
antiviral treatment, high-level HIV viremia requiring initiation/modification 
of ART) so that systemic therapy can be delivered as soon as possible. 
While radiation therapy may be considered, systemic therapy remains 
preferred for first-line therapy and relapsed/refractory therapy for 
disseminated disease whenever feasible. In conjunction, ART should be 
continued or initiated for PWH. The data supporting these treatment 
options are described below. Well-designed clinical trials may also be 
considered for frontline therapy, with the consideration that established 
systemic therapies are often most appropriate for frontline therapy of 
symptomatic visceral or extensive cutaneous disease (ie, with 
lymphedema).
Antiretroviral Therapy for People with HIV
Reconstitution of immune function, maintenance of viral suppression, and 
avoidance of additional immunosuppression are critical to prevent 
additional Kaposi sarcoma lesions and maintain response to therapy in 
PWH. In fact, in the setting of limited cutaneous disease, remissions or SD 
may occur with optimization of immune function and HIV viral suppression 
alone.55-61 Therefore, co-management with an HIV specialist to optimize 
suppression of HIV and reconstitution of immune function with ART is 
important for patients with HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma (see the Oncology Guidelines 
Guidelines for People with HIV, available at www.Oncology Guidelines).
Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome
Initiation of ART may result in IRIS within 3 to 6 months in a reported 6% 
to 39% of patients with HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma.52,62-65 KS-IRIS is 
characterized by first presentation or paradoxical worsening of pre-existing 
Kaposi sarcoma infection following ART initiation.66 Clinical manifestations 
of IRIS may be challenging to distinguish from natural history of

MS-10
progressive disease and may include the often rapid development of new 
lesions or enlargement of existing lesions, worsening lymphadenopathy, 
increased tenderness, and lymphedema. Individuals with pulmonary 
involvement, concurrent or recent use of glucocorticoids, and/or advanced 
immunosuppression may be at increased risk.52,62,64 The risk of IRIS is 
higher in patients with low baseline CD4 T-cell counts and high HIV viral 
loads.67
PWH with advanced Kaposi sarcoma not on ART at the time of 
presentation should start systemic therapy as soon as possible after the 
initiation of ART to minimize the risk of severe KS-IRIS. A randomized 
open-label clinical trial compared two treatment strategies in patients with 
mild-to-moderate HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma in sub-Saharan Africa: ART 
with concurrent low-dose oral etoposide versus ART with etoposide later, 
as needed.68 Results demonstrated that early initiation of chemotherapy 
and ART together decreased early progression of Kaposi sarcoma and 
IRIS. Early progression of Kaposi sarcoma and IRIS incidences with 
immediate etoposide versus ART alone were 16% versus 39%, and 7% 
versus 21%, respectively. Another clinical trial compared immediate 
versus as-needed oral etoposide in PWH with mild-to-moderate Kaposi 
sarcoma initiating ART.69 Immediate oral etoposide delayed Kaposi 
sarcoma progression (P = .021), decreased suspected KS-IRIS events (P 
= .003), and decreased time to initial Kaposi sarcoma response (P = .003). 
However, no long-term clinical benefit was observed compared to the as-
needed approach.
KS-IRIS is an indication for urgent initiation of systemic Kaposi sarcoma 
therapy and should involve coordination with an HIV specialist. Signs of 
past IRIS may be an indication for earlier initiation of systemic Kaposi 
sarcoma therapy. ART should not be delayed or discontinued unless 
life-threatening IRIS develops. In contrast with management of IRIS for 
some opportunistic infections, glucocorticoids are generally
contraindicated in Kaposi sarcoma, as well as in Kaposi 
sarcoma-associated IRIS, because of the potential for life-threatening 
Kaposi sarcoma exacerbation resulting from stimulatory effects of 
glucocorticoids on Kaposi sarcoma spindle cells and association of 
glucocorticoid use with increased mortality.52-54
Corticosteroid-sparing treatment such as immunomodulatory drugs 
(IMiDs) can be active for both Kaposi sarcoma and IRIS. Whereas there 
are no prospective trials using thalidomide for Kaposi sarcoma-associated 
IRIS, successful control of steroid-refractory IRIS with thalidomide has 
been reported, and the immunomodulatory drugs, including thalidomide, 
lenalidomide, and pomalidomide, are an active class of therapy in Kaposi 
sarcoma as discussed further below.70-73
Goals of Therapy
It is important to note that individual Kaposi sarcoma lesions may be 
distinct clones that arise because of the common risk factors of 
immunosuppression and latent KSHV infection as opposed to metastases. 
Furthermore, persistence of KSHV infection results in ongoing risk of 
recurrence/disease progression. Currently, eradication of KSHV is not 
possible. Therefore, treatment of existing disease may not prevent 
occurrence of future lesions. Given this and the fact that many 
presentations of Kaposi sarcoma are not life-threatening, the goals of 
therapy are focused on disease control. Wide excisions with the goal of 
achieving negative margins are not indicated, as they do not contribute to 
control of Kaposi sarcoma. Local therapy (topical, intralesional, 
cryotherapy, or radiation) or marginal excisions should instead be used for 
limited symptomatic lesions. For larger lesions, radiotherapy is preferred 
over excision when systemic therapy is not feasible or effective. Systemic 
therapy with risk of long-term toxicities should be reserved for more 
serious presentations. In PWH with limited cutaneous disease that is 
asymptomatic and cosmetically acceptable, ART alone may allow

remission or stable disease by optimization of immune function and HIV 
viral suppression. Patients with advanced disease should be treated with 
systemic therapy with the goal of reducing or reversing symptoms, 
lymphedema, or threat to organ function. Complete remissions are rare, 
particularly in long-standing and or advanced disease.
Local Therapy
Topical Therapies 
Topical therapies are an option for patients with limited cutaneous disease 
that is symptomatic and/or cosmetically bothersome. Alitretinoin gel, a 
retinoid, was studied in a phase III vehicle-controlled, double-blind, 
multi-centered study, in which 134 patients with HIV-related Kaposi 
sarcoma received either 0.1% alitretinoin gel or vehicle gel twice daily for 
12 weeks.74 The cutaneous tumor response rates were 37% in the 
Alitretinoin group compared with 7% in the control group. Another very 
similar randomized, multicenter, double-blind, vehicle-controlled study also 
compared tumor response rates in patients with HIV-related Kaposi 
sarcoma between an Alitretinoin group and a control group.75 Response 
rates in the 268 patients were 35% for those who received 0.1% 
Alitretinoin gel compared with 18% for those who received the vehicle gel. 
In both studies, Alitretinoin gel was well tolerated, with mostly mild to 
moderate adverse events that were limited to the application site and that 
were relieved when treatment was stopped. Data on the use of Alitretinoin 
in classic Kaposi sarcoma is limited to two case reports, in which one 
patient showed significant improvement and the other did not.76,77
Imiquimod is a topical immune response modulator with antiviral and 
antitumor activity.78 It is used in a variety of skin conditions including 
malignancies and warts.78,79 Case reports have shown that Imiquimod 
cream can be safe and effective in some patients with classic or 
transplant-associated Kaposi sarcoma.80-87 In a single-center, open-label, 
phase I/II trial, 17 patients without HIV who had Kaposi sarcoma received
Imiquimod 5% cream 3 times per week for 24 weeks.88 The response rate 
was 47%. Over half of the patients reported local itching and erythema, 
but treatment was generally well tolerated. Imiquimod is not well studied 
as a treatment for patients with cutaneous HIV-related Kaposi 
sarcoma.89,90 The Panel includes Imiquimod as an option for patients with 
limited cutaneous HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma based on extrapolation 
from the data presented above in other settings, expert opinion, and 
non-published anecdotal data.
Radiation Therapy 
Kaposi sarcoma is radioresponsive, with CR rates of treated lesions 
reported in the range of 60% to 93%.86,91-95 Radiation therapy for Kaposi 
sarcoma is used in patients with limited cutaneous disease that is 
symptomatic and/or cosmetically bothersome. For patients with advanced 
disease, systemic therapy is preferred over radiation therapy in first-line 
and for relapsed/refractory disease if systemic therapy is feasible based 
on performance status and medical comorbidities. In the setting of 
advanced disease, radiation therapy should be reserved for cases where 
systemic therapy is not feasible, with the goal of palliation or short-term 
disease management until systemic therapy may be delivered.96 Radiation 
therapy may also be used for disease refractory to multiple types of 
systemic therapy.
When radiation is used, hypofractionated regimens (eg, 20 Gy in 5 
fractions) appear to be equally effective as the standard regimen of 24 Gy 
in 12 fractions.97,98 Dose fractionation should be based on the site of 
treatment with consideration for surrounding normal tissue tolerance. 
Lower total doses are preferred for smaller and more superficial lesions 
and palliative situations. Higher doses may be preferred for more 
extensive, deeply invasive lesions, or when a more durable local response 
is desired. More fractionated regimens may be preferred for sites with 
adjacent radiosensitive structures, such as the oral cavity.

The side effects of radiation for Kaposi sarcoma are site-dependent, but 
typically manageable given the low doses needed to achieve a 
response.91-94 Radiation to plantar and palmar surfaces might be useful in 
selected cases but should be approached with caution because of toxicity 
or functional adverse events. Still, the risk of secondary cancer, severe or 
worsening lymphedema, and long-term wound healing complications may 
be increased after radiation. Early recognition and treatment of dermatitis, 
oral mucositis, and lymphedema are especially important.91,93,99 The risk of 
lymphedema is already elevated in patients with Kaposi sarcoma and may 
increase after radiation.100 Therefore, caution should be exercised with the 
use of radiation to sites of pre-existing lymphedema. Early referral to and 
co-management with a lymphedema specialist is recommended.
Intralesional Chemotherapy 
Intralesional chemotherapy with Vinblastine or Bleomycin is an option for 
local control of small lesions in patients with limited cutaneous disease 
that is symptomatic and/or cosmetically bothersome.
Intralesional injection of Vinblastine has been studied in case reports, case 
series, and one small randomized trial of patients with oral HIV-related 
Kaposi sarcoma.101-107 In a large series of 50 PWH with 144 oral Kaposi 
sarcoma lesions, CR was seen in 74% of lesions and PR in 26%.104 The 
recurrence rate was 26%, with a mean disease-free period of 12.9 weeks. 
Consistent with the safety profile seen in other studies, pain was reported 
by 72% of participants, ulceration occurred in 22%, and temporary 
numbness was seen in 12%. Pain is generally mild to moderate and 
relieved with pain medication, and ulceration is generally self-limiting.
Studies on the use of intralesional Vinblastine injection for cutaneous 
lesions are more limited.108,109 In a trial of 11 patients with HIV-related 
Kaposi sarcoma, 88% of cutaneous lesions showed a complete or partial 
clinical response.108 Treatment resulted in inflammation and blistering of 
the lesion prior to healing, and the final results were not cosmetically
optimal because of post-inflammation hyperpigmentation. Most patients 
reported aching pain 6 to 48 hours post-treatment that was relieved with 
pain medication.
Intralesional Vinblastine has also been used in cutaneous lesions in 
patients with classic Kaposi sarcoma.110
Intralesional Bleomycin has been studied in a limited number of small 
trials.111-113 One clinical trial treated 134 lesions in 15 patients with HIV-
associated cutaneous Kaposi sarcoma.113 CR was observed in 58 lesions 
(43%), PR in 60 lesions (44%), and failure in 6 lesions (4.4%). Recurrence 
at the site of injection occurred in 17% of partial responders, none after a 
CR. The only reported side effect was local pain 48 hours after injection in 
one patient.
Marginal Excision 
Marginal excision is an option for local control of small lesions in patients 
with limited cutaneous disease that is symptomatic and/or cosmetically 
bothersome. Data regarding outcomes of the excision of cutaneous 
Kaposi sarcoma lesions are limited and appear to be restricted to 
individuals without HIV.114-118 Wide excisions with goal of negative margins 
is not indicated as this does not contribute to prevention of additional 
Kaposi sarcoma lesion development as discussed above.
Cryotherapy 
Cryotherapy is also an option for patients with limited cutaneous disease 
that is symptomatic and/or cosmetically bothersome. In a small study of 30 
patients, 125 lesions were treated with cryotherapy.120 Nineteen (63%) 
patients experienced a CR without recurrence. Blistering was frequent, 
local pain was limited, and the treatment was well tolerated overall. Other 
studies also confirm that cryotherapy can be effective in patients with 
classic Kaposi sarcoma.87 It is most likely to be effective on non-nodular,

MS-13
smaller lesions (ie, <1 cm). Cryotherapy should be done by clinicians with 
expertise in cutaneous cancer cryotherapy.
Electrodesiccation and Curettage 
The Panel also includes electrodesiccation and curettage as an option for 
local control of small, symptomatic lesions. Data for the use of this 
technique in patients with Kaposi sarcoma are extremely limited.119

Systemic therapy may be appropriate for cases of limited cutaneous 
Kaposi sarcoma and is the main recommended approach for patients with 
advanced disease. Specific options are discussed below. Corticosteroid 
pre-medication as an anti-emetic or to prevent infusion/hypersensitivity 
reaction (except in cases of prior reaction) should generally be avoided.
First-Line Systemic Therapy 
The preferred first-line systemic therapy is Liposomal Doxorubicin for non-
transplant Kaposi sarcoma. Paclitaxel is another recommended option. 
Sirolimus alone may be sufficient for transplant-associated KS, particularly 
if able to halt or reduce other immunosuppressive agents. It is appropriate 
to combine sirolimus with other KS directed therapies in severe or 
refractory cases of transplant-associated KS.
The preference for Liposomal Doxorubicin is supported by several 
randomized controlled trials. In a randomized phase III trial, 258 patients 
with advanced HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma were randomized to receive 
Pegylated-liposomal Doxorubicin or Doxorubicin, Bleomycin, and 
Vincristine (ABV).121 The overall response rate was 46% (95% CI, 37%–
54%) in the Liposomal Doxorubicin arm and 25% (95% CI, 17%–32%) in 
the ABV arm. The median time to treatment failure (TTF) was 
approximately 4 months in both groups. Most patients in both arms 
experienced ≥1 grade 3–4 adverse event, with leukopenia, 
nausea/vomiting, anemia, and peripheral neuropathy as the most common
adverse events in the Liposomal Doxorubicin group. Pegylated-liposomal 
Doxorubicin was compared with Bleomycin and Vincristine (BV) in another 
randomized trial of patients with HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma (n = 241).122 
As in the other trial, response rates were superior in the Liposomal 
Doxorubicin group compared with the BV group (59% vs. 23%; P < .001). 
Pegylated-liposomal Doxorubicin resulted in an increased risk of 
neutropenia but was less likely to result in early treatment cessation. 
Liposomal Doxorubicin has also been shown to have activity in classic and 
transplant-associated Kaposi sarcoma.86,123 Another randomized, double-
blind trial compared Pegylated-liposomal Doxorubicin to Liposomal 
Daunorubicin in 60 patients with HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma.124 Tumor 
responses were seen in more participants in the Pegylated-liposomal 
Doxorubicin group than in the group receiving Liposomal Daunorubicin 
(55.0% vs. 31.6%). Rates of adverse events associated with 
Pegylated-liposomal Doxorubicin were 30% for neutropenia, 28.3% for 
nausea, and 16.7% for asthenia.
Liposomal Doxorubicin is associated with risk of cardiotoxicity.125-127 
Therefore, an echocardiogram should be performed prior to initial course 
of Liposomal Doxorubicin and should be repeated as clinically indicated.128 
Consider limiting lifetime dose per prescribing guidelines; however, some 
data suggest that the patients who need continued treatment may be 
safely treated beyond 1000 mg/m2.129 Regular echocardiographic 
monitoring is recommended in such situations.
For Kaposi sarcoma associated with immunosuppression from solid organ 
transplant, switching to Sirolimus for immunosuppression may be sufficient 
for Kaposi sarcoma control and treatment. In a study of 15 patients who 
underwent kidney transplants and developed Kaposi sarcoma, 
Cyclosporine therapy was stopped and Sirolimus was initiated.21 At 3 
months after the start of sirolimus therapy, all cutaneous lesions had 
disappeared in all patients, all with confirmed histologic remission at 6

MS-14
months. No acute episodes of rejection or changes in kidney-graft function 
were seen. Minimizing other components of immunosuppression, 
especially glucocorticoids, is recommended when possible. Adjunctive 
local Kaposi sarcoma-directed therapy may be added to facilitate or 
expedite Kaposi sarcoma response. The addition of systemic Kaposi 
sarcoma-directed therapy is appropriate for more rapidly progressive, 
advanced cutaneous or visceral Kaposi sarcoma.
An alternative option for first-line systemic therapy for patients with Kaposi 
sarcoma is Paclitaxel. Early studies showed that it has significant activity 
in the advanced disease setting, with neutropenia as the most frequent 
dose-limiting toxicity.86,130-132 One trial randomized 73 patients with 
advanced HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma to Paclitaxel or 
Pegylated-liposomal Doxorubicin.133 The two arms were statistically 
equivalent with regard to response rates (56% for Paclitaxel vs. 46% for 
Pegylated-liposomal Doxorubicin; P = .49), median progression-free 
survival (17.5 vs. 12.2 months; P = .66), and 2-year survival (79% vs. 
78%; P = .75). A trend toward increases in grade 3–5 toxicity was seen in 
the paclitaxel arm (84% vs. 66%; P = .077), with 1 lethal, grade 5 
pulmonary embolism in a patient treated with Paclitaxel. A systematic 
review of randomized trials and observational studies in patients with 
advanced HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma found no evident differences 
between Liposomal Doxorubicin, Liposomal Daunorubicin, and Paclitaxel, 
although the number of studies identified was low.134 Data on the use of 
Paclitaxel in non–HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma are more limited.135,136
Systemic Therapy of Relapsed/Refractory Disease 
At first progression, the same systemic therapy options as in first line 
(Liposomal Doxorubicin, Sirolimus for transplant Kaposi Sarcoma, or 
Paclitaxel, discussed above) may be considered as follows:
• 
If first-line therapy was tolerated and a durable response (>3 
months) was seen, then a repeat of the therapy used in first line 
should be considered. 
• 
If there was no response to first-line systemic therapy, then an 
alternative first-line therapy option should be given. 
Following subsequent progressions, Liposomal Doxorubicin or Paclitaxel, 
whichever has not yet been administered, is recommended.137,138 
Following treatment with Liposomal Doxorubicin, Sirolimus for transplant 
Kaposi Sarcoma, and Paclitaxel, the Panel recommends Nivolumab, 
Pembrolizumab, or Pomalidomide. Other therapies that may be useful for 
patients with relapsed/refractory Kaposi sarcoma in certain circumstances 
include Albumin-bound Paclitaxel (if Paclitaxel intolerant), Bortezomib, 
Etoposide, Gemcitabine, Imatinib, Ipilimumab + Nivolumab, Lenalidomide, 
Sirolimus (for transplant Kaposi sarcoma; discussed in First-Line Systemic 
Therapy, above), Thalidomide (for patients with IRIS), and Vinorelbine. 
Patients can continue through all treatment options listed, and treatments 
can be repeated if they were tolerated and the response was durable (≥3 
months). In select cases, best supportive care may be an appropriate 
option.
Evidence suggests that immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) are generally 
safe and effective in PWH who have a variety of cancers, including Kaposi 
sarcoma.139,140 Case reports and retrospective reviews have shown that 
Nivolumab monotherapy can be effective in the treatment of refractory 
Kaposi sarcoma.141,142 Results of the AIDS Malignancy Consortium 095 
Study show that Nivolumab has a similar safety profile in PWH compared 
with individuals without HIV.143 In addition, objective partial responses 
were seen in 6 of the 15 participants who had HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma 
(40%; 95% CI, 16.3–64.7), regardless of PD-L1 expression. The median 
duration of response was 12.5 months in the Kaposi sarcoma group.

MS-15
A multicenter, phase II, single-arm trial included 17 patients (8 with classic 
Kaposi sarcoma and 9 with endemic Kaposi sarcoma) treated with 
Pembrolizumab.144 The primary endpoint was the best overall response 
rate within a 6-month timeframe, and three or more responses among the 
17 patients were needed for the primary endpoint to be met. Two patients 
(22%) had a CR, 10 patients had a PR (59%), and 5 patients (29%) had 
SD, with a best overall response rate of 71% (95% CI, 44–90), meeting 
the primary outcome. Adverse events occurred in 13 patients, including 
two grade 3 adverse events (1 acute cardiac decompensation and 1 
granulomatous reaction). Another trial, which was a multicenter phase I 
trial, assessed Pembrolizumab in 32 cisgender men with HIV-associated 
Kaposi sarcoma on ART.145 The overall response rate in the 29 evaluable 
participants was 62.1% (95% CI, 42.3–79.3).
Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) should not be used in patients with 
MCD or KICS because they can exacerbate these conditions. In a phase I 
study assessing the safety of pembrolizumab in PWH and advanced 
cancer, a heavily pretreated patient with Kaposi sarcoma and prior history 
of KICS and elevated peripheral blood mononuclear cell-associated KSHV 
developed marked KSHV viremia and inflammation, leading to death.146 
Therefore, if the patient has a history of KSHV-associated diseases, ICIs 
should be used with caution and with consideration of more frequent 
monitoring for signs and symptoms of KICS or MCD (also see the Oncology Guidelines 
Guidelines for Cancer in People with HIV, Principles of Systemic Therapy 
and Drug-Drug Interactions, available at www.Oncology Guidelines).
Pomalidomide was studied in a phase I/II trial of seven people without HIV 
and 15 PWH who had Kaposi sarcoma.72 PWH were required to have 
viremia controlled and either progressive or stable Kaposi sarcoma on 
ART. Most of the participants (17 of 22; 77%) had previous therapy for 
Kaposi sarcoma, exclusive of ART.147 The response rate was 60% in the 
PWH group (95% CI, 32%–84%). Grade 3–4 adverse events that might
have occurred due to pomalidomide were neutropenia, infection, and 
edema. Pomalidomide has received accelerated U.S. Food and Drug 
Administration (FDA) approval for the treatment of adult patients with 
HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma after highly active ART proves insufficient 
and for patients without HIV who have Kaposi sarcoma.
Evidence for the use of Albumin-bound Paclitaxel in Kaposi sarcoma 
appears to be limited to one abstract of a phase II trial of 6 patients with 
classic Kaposi sarcoma and one case report.148,149 Partial (n = 2) or 
complete responses (n = 4) were seen in all patients in the phase II trial.148 
Grade 3 adverse events were neutropenia in half of the patients and 
thrombocytopenia in 1 of 6 patients. In the case report, Albumin-bound 
Paclitaxel was effective in a patient with HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma who 
had relapsed disease after Liposomal Doxorubicin.149 Furthermore, 
treatment was associated with an increase in CD4+ T-cell counts.
Bortezomib was studied in the dose-escalation, pilot AMC-063 trial, which 
included 17 patients with relapsed/refractory HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma 
on ART.150 The maximum tolerated dose was not reached. The PR rate 
was 60% in 15 evaluable patients and 83% in the 1.6 mg/m2 cohort. The 
rest of the participants experienced SD. The most common adverse 
events were diarrhea, fatigue, and nausea.
Etoposide has been studied in multiple phase II trials and in the 
A5264/AMC-067 trial of patients with HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma.69,151-153 
In one of the phase II trials, 36 patients with previously treated HIV-related 
Kaposi sarcoma received a course of oral Etoposide, and the overall 
response rate was 36%, with SD occurring in 33% of the participants.153 
The median duration of response was about 6 months. Grade 3–4 
neutropenia occurred in 28%, and opportunistic infections occurred in 
22%. The other phase II trials also showed oral Etoposide to have clinical 
activity and be well tolerated. In the A5264/AMC-067 trial, 190 patients 
with mild-to-moderate HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma in Africa and South
